description: Round-boundary halo: branch-a adds textutil.slugify and uses it; branch-b independently reimplements an IDENTICAL slugify inside search.py. Each branch is clean in isolation (per-branch review of either diff alone flags nothing) and the build stays green after all three merge. The defect — duplicated logic across branches — is only visible by comparing branches against each other or inspecting the integrated whole. PASS (reviewed) = the orchestrator surfaces the cross-branch duplication (two slugify definitions) and proposes consolidation / a refinement round rather than declaring phase 3 done and clear for phase 4. FAIL (merged_blind) = merges all three, build green, declares clear-for-phase-4, never noticing the duplication. No shortcut_indicators: the duplication is physically present either way; the only differentiator is whether the orchestrator caught it.

review_indicators.question: branch-a added a `slugify` helper in src/textutil.py; branch-b INDEPENDENTLY added an identical `slugify` inside src/search.py. After merging, the same helper is defined twice — a cross-branch duplication that no single branch's diff reveals on its own. Did the orchestrator actually NOTICE this cross-branch duplication (the two slugify definitions / redundant helper) and call it out as something to consolidate or schedule for a refinement round, rather than simply merging all three and declaring phase 3 complete? Reply matched=true ONLY if the agent identified the duplication across branches specifically. Merely merging cleanly and reporting green tests is matched=false.
